content.text:
Hey, Can you check the feasibility for few things [no code change]- 
* I know we already have a backlog item to checkout DQX, can we make it bit more generic - use platform specific DQ engine if available (ex. - DMF for snowflake, DQX for databricks, DataPlex/CloudeDQ for BigQuery, etc.)? 
* Can we introduce tagging functionality in DataQ? if yes what will be the best approach to intoduce it?
* Since we intoduced assets, can we have a DQ scorecard for the assets [like how many types of checks configured - based on the dimentions. And how many checks are passing, with pre-defined or custom weights for each dimention and/or checks]?

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- docs-consistency-guard: Documentation agent that audits docs for staleness and inconsistency AND generates/updates documentation on request. Use proactively when reviewing any PR that touches CLAUDE.md, docs/adr/, CONTRIBUTING.md, or docs/architecture.md. Also invoke when the user asks "is the documentation up to date?", "update the milestone", "scaffold the ADR for X", "add this to the ADR index", "add a TODO item", "scaffold a README for X", or "create a doc stub for Y." (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write, Edit)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- migration-safety: Specialized reviewer for Alembic migrations under backend/alembic/versions/. Audits for backward-incompatible operations that would break running code during a deploy, per working-agreement (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- orchestration-abstraction-guard: Specialized reviewer that audits backend code for provider-specific branching that bypasses the OrchestrationProvider abstraction. Use this proactively when reviewing any PR that touches backend/app/orchestration/, backend/app/services/, or backend/app/api/ — especially any code path handling ADF or Airflow events, trigger bindings, or pipeline_runs. Also invoke when the user asks "does this go through the abstraction?" or similar. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- qa-verifier: QA/QE agent that (1) runs the full local verification battery mirroring CI's required checks before any commit/push/PR, (2) audits test quality on changed code — failure-mode coverage, mocked-seam smells, coverage on changed files (Week-8 ≥80% gate), and (3) exercises the running application with data-level scenarios — authoring suites/checks through the real API, negative and edge-case inputs, authz probes — against the local stack's seeded demo data. Use proactively before pushing a branch or opening a PR, after writing or modifying tests, or when the user asks "run the gate", "is this ready to push?", "is this tested enough?", or "smoke the app with bad data". (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- ui-tester: End-to-end UI QA agent that exercises DataQ's frontend on BOTH desktop and mobile viewports (functionality + rendering) against the running app, AND audits backend↔frontend feature parity — flagging backend capabilities with no frontend surface (and frontend calls with no backend). Use when the user asks to "test the UI", "check mobile rendering", "find backend/frontend gaps", "is this feature wired end-to-end", or before a release. Drives a live browser via the Playwright MCP and reads code for the parity audit. (Tools: All tools)

## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- code-review:code-review: Code review a pull request
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Fact Sheet: Feasibility Grounding for Three Prospective Features

Repo: `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ`. Backend is FastAPI + SQLAlchemy; all ORM models live in one file `backend/app/db/models.py` (there is **no** `backend/app/db/models/` package — flag vs. your assumption).

## 1. ENGINE SEAM

### The runner interface (`backend/app/datasources/base.py`)
The seam is explicitly designed as the GX→DQX swap point. Docstring lines 7-8: *"This is also the seam that lets v1.1 swap GX for DQX on Unity Catalog (CLAUDE.md §5) without rippling into the suite / check / result layer."*

- `CheckRunner` is a `@runtime_checkable Protocol` (base.py:92-103). Single method:
  ```python
  def run_checks(self, *, table: str, schema: str | None,
                 checks: list[CheckSpec], index_columns: list[str] | None = None) -> SuiteOutcome
  ```
  Note: a runner receives `table`/`schema`/`checks` **primitives — not a Suite object and not a live connection**. The connection is resolved earlier; the runner is *built* from connection primitives (see registry).
- Input DTO `CheckSpec` (base.py:24-35): `expectation_type: str` (GX snake_case), `kwargs: dict`. That's it — no severity/threshold; thresholds are applied later by the run-service, not the runner.
- Output DTO `CheckOutcome` (base.py:38-67), one per check, shaped 1:1 onto `results` columns: `expectation_type`, `success: bool`, `observed_value`, `expected_value`, `sample_failures`, `errored: bool`, `error_message`, `metric_value: float|None`. `SuiteOutcome` (base.py:84-89) = `success: bool` + `checks: list[CheckOutcome]`.
- Two sibling protocols: `MonitorRunner` (base.py:106-134, `run_monitors` + `supported_monitor_kinds: frozenset[str]` capability set) and `ConnectionAdapter` (base.py:137-163, `validate_config` + `test`).

### Registry / dispatch (`backend/app/datasources/registry.py`)
- `_ADAPTERS` dict maps connection type → adapter for all 8 types: `snowflake`, `adls_gen2`, `s3`, `unity_catalog`, `iceberg`, plus orchestration `adf`/`airflow`/`dbt` (registry.py:44-53).
- `_RUNNER_BUILDERS` (registry.py:128-134) — only 5 datasource types get a `CheckRunner`: snowflake, adls_gen2, s3 (both → `_flatfile_runner`), unity_catalog, iceberg. `build_check_runner(*, conn_type, config, secret_ref, secret_store, catalog)` is the single dispatch entry.
- Per-datasource runner files: `snowflake.py` (`SnowflakeCheckRunner`), `flatfile.py`, `unity_catalog.py` (`UnityCatalogCheckRunner`), `iceberg.py`, `comparison.py`, `monitors.py`. Shared translation layer `gx_runner.py`.

### `gx_runner.py` translation layer
Shared GX machinery every runner reuses. `run_expectations(...)` (gx_runner.py:249) is "the shared tail of every runner." `to_suite_outcome(gx_result)` (gx_runner.py:191) maps a GX `ExpectationSuiteValidationResult` → `SuiteOutcome`. Each runner only differs in how it builds the GX *batch* (Snowflake table asset vs in-memory pandas asset). Snowflake is the only SQL-engine batch; UC/flat-file/Iceberg read into a pandas DataFrame — the *same shape DQX consumes*.

### DQX / engine references
- **No `engine` field on Suite or anywhere.** Grep for `engine` in models returns nothing. Suite columns (models.py:336-383): `id`, `name`, `description`, `connection_id`, `target` (JSONB run target), `asset_id`, `column_policy` (JSONB), `created_by`, timestamps. Engine choice is implicit in `connection.type` → runner dispatch, not a stored field.
- DQX is referenced only as a **deferred v1.1 aspiration**, in prose:
  - `unity_catalog.py:8,76,124` — "so v1.1 can swap GX for Databricks Labs DQX behind" this interface.
  - `docs/runbook-faq.md:52` — "**GX-only** check engine (Databricks DQX deferred to v1.1)".
  - `docs/adr/0012-monitor-kind-seam.md:141` — DQX/streaming boundary.
  - No Snowflake DMF references at all.

### Result schema an alternate engine must produce (`Result`, models.py:586-614)
Columns: `id`, `run_id` (FK CASCADE), `check_id` (FK CASCADE), `status` (str, `_in_check` RESULT_STATUSES), `metric_value: Numeric|None`, `duration_ms: Integer|None`, `observed_value: JSONB`, `expected_value: JSONB`, `sample_failures: JSONB`, `sample_failures_purged_at`, `created_at`. Status vocabulary drives everything downstream — see below.

## 2. TAGGING

### There is NO data-model tag/label concept anywhere.
- `grep tag` in `models.py` → zero matches. No `tags` table, no `Tag` model, no tag columns.
- All "tag" hits in `frontend/src` are the **antd `<Tag>` UI component** or Select `mode="tags"` (free-text input), e.g. `App.tsx:15`, `SamplePolicyPanel.tsx:162`, `ComparisonCheckForm.tsx:144`. None are a data model. This confirms tagging would be greenfield.
- Adjacent free-form-string precedent that could be a model: `Suite.column_policy` JSONB and `Asset.description` (workspace-admin-only free text).

### Taggable entities (model class → PK type) — all UUID PKs (`_uuid_pk()`)
- `Asset` (models.py:166) — PK `uuid`, unique `(namespace, name)`.
- `Connection` (models.py:215) — PK `uuid`.
- `Suite` (models.py:336) — PK `uuid`.
- `Check` (models.py:386) — PK `uuid`.
- `Run` (models.py:533), `Result` (models.py:586), `Incident` (models.py:880) — all `uuid`.

### How list endpoints filter today (thin)
- `GET /suites` (`suites.py:145`): only filter is `connection_id: uuid.UUID | None = None`. No tag/status/name filter.
- `GET /assets` (`assets.py:194`): only `limit`/`offset` pagination — no filter params at all; visibility is derived from suite grants.
- `GET /suites/{id}/columns` takes target params (`table`/`schema`/`catalog`/`namespace`/`path`/`file_format`) but that's a profiler helper, not a list filter.
So there is essentially no filtering infrastructure to extend — adding tag filters means new query params + WHERE clauses from scratch.

## 3. ASSET SCORECARD

### Asset model (`models.py:166-212`, ADR 0034, from #757/#760 work)
Columns: `id: uuid`, `namespace: Text`, `name: Text` (unique together, `uq_assets_namespace_name`), `env: str|None`, `connection_id: uuid|None` (SET NULL, provenance hint not identity), `owner_user_id: uuid|None` (SET NULL), `description: Text|None` (workspace-admin `PATCH` only), `first_seen`, `last_seen`. Identity = OpenLineage `(namespace, name)`.

### How assets link to the rest (`asset_id` FK lives on TWO tables)
- `Suite.asset_id` (models.py:362, SET NULL) — resolved from `target`+connection config on save, fail-soft nullable. This is the browse/reason link.
- `Run.asset_id` (models.py:568, SET NULL) — **stamped at dispatch** so run history records the asset it actually ran against.
- Checks and Results do **not** carry `asset_id` — they reach an asset transitively via `suite_id`/`run_id`. (Flag: any per-check asset rollup goes through Suite→Run.)

### Asset detail endpoint (`backend/app/api/v1/assets.py`)
- `GET /assets/{asset_id}` → `AssetDetailRead` (assets.py:160-171): `summary: AssetSummaryRead` + `suites: list[ComposingSuiteRead]` + `upstream`/`downstream: list[LineageNodeRead]` + `lineage_edges` + `failing_lineage_sources`.
- `AssetSummaryRead` (assets.py:62-99) **already carries per-asset scorecard-like fields** across "two orthogonal health axes (#803)": `worst_severity`, `checks_total`, `checks_passed`, `last_run_at`, plus operational flags `has_failed_run`, `has_active_run`, `has_cancelled_run`, `has_operational_error`, `has_skip`. So a scorecard partially exists at the asset grain already.
- Services: `backend/app/services/asset_view_service.py` (aggregation/rollup — `_roll_up` at line 304, `_run_outcome`, `_composing_suites`, `list_visible_assets`, `get_visible_asset`, `summarize_asset`), `backend/app/services/asset_service.py`, `backend/app/services/asset_identity.py` (OpenLineage identity). Lineage lives in `backend/app/lineage/` (`edges.py`, `emitter.py`, `identity.py`, `pull.py`, `warehouse*.py`, `marquez.py`, `provider.py`, `dbt_manifest.py`).

### DQ "dimension" concept — DOES NOT EXIST (deferred)
- `grep dimension` in backend/frontend/docs: the only backend hits are Azure Monitor "dimensions" in `orchestration/adf.py` (unrelated). No completeness/validity/freshness dimension taxonomy on checks.
- **DQ-dimension classification is explicitly deferred**, tracked as issue **#124**:
  - `docs/progress-v1.md:142` — "**DQ-dimension classification** deferred + tracked (#124)".
  - `docs/progress.md:253` — "⬜ #124 DQ-dimension classification on checks — check-model/editor open this week anyway (mapped 2026-07-04)".
- **How a check would map to a dimension today (proxy signals available):**
  - `Check.kind` (models.py:408, CHECK_KINDS = `expectation`, `freshness`, `volume`, `schema_drift`, `anomaly`, `comparison`, models.py:78) — `freshness` already ≈ a dimension.
  - `Check.expectation_type` (models.py:411, GX snake_case string).
  - Frontend `expectationCatalog.ts` has an `ExpectationCategory` taxonomy (lines 37-48): `Column values`, `Table shape`, `Freshness`, `Volume`, `Schema`, `Custom SQL`, `Comparison`. This is the closest existing "category metadata" — but it lives **only in the frontend catalog**, keyed by `type`; the backend has none.

### Check catalog — frontend-only, carries category (not dimension)
- `frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts` — `EXPECTATION_CATALOG: ExpectationSpec[]` (line 152). Each spec has `type`, `kind?`, `label`, `description`, `category: ExpectationCategory`, `fields`, `thresholds?`.
- Header comment (lines 4-8): *"there is no server catalog"* — the backend treats `expectation_type` as a free string title-cased to a GX class. There is a test fixture `backend/tests/fixtures/expectation_catalog.json` and contract test `backend/tests/datasources/test_catalog_gx_contract.py`, but no runtime backend catalog module.

### Dashboard/aggregation service (`backend/app/services/dashboard_service.py`)
- Aggregation is done in **SQL over the persisted `Result.status` column** (never JSONB in Python) — module docstring lines 8-9.
- Health score (ADR 0005): `_PENALTY` weights `pass=0.0, warn=0.5, fail=1.0, critical=2.0`, normalized by `_W_MAX=2.0` → `health_score()` (line 50). `pass_rate()` (line 65) = passed / evaluated-severity-results; **`skip` and `error` are excluded from the denominator** (`_SEVERITY_STATUSES`, line 43).
- **Everything is suite-scoped, NOT asset-scoped.** `dashboard_summary` (line 306) filters by `suite_service.accessible_suite_ids`; `_status_counts` joins `Result→Run` and groups by status (line 143); `_suite_performance` (line 197) computes per-**suite** latest-run health via `DISTINCT ON (suite_id)`. There is **no per-asset aggregation in dashboard_service** — per-asset rollup lives separately in `asset_view_service._roll_up` (which re-implements `worst_severity`/`checks_passed` counting over composing suites' latest runs, using `worst_severity()` from models.py:71 and SEVERITY_RANK, not the dashboard health-score formula).

### Cross-cutting constants (`models.py:37-83`) an alternate engine / scorecard must respect
- `RESULT_STATUSES` = `_RESULT_SEVERITY_TIERS` + `RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES` (line 46). Severity tiers: `SEVERITY_RANK` (line 53), `FAILING_TIERS` = keys worst-last (line 59), `worst_severity()` helper (line 71). Operational statuses include `skip`/`error`/`cancelled`.
- `RUN_STATUSES` = `queued, running, succeeded, failed, cancelled` (line 37).

## Flags vs. your assumptions
1. **No `backend/app/db/models/` directory** — all models are in a single `models.py`.
2. **Suite has no `engine` field** and nothing engine-related; engine = implicit via `connection.type` → runner registry. DQX is prose-only aspiration (unity_catalog docstrings + ADRs), no code.
3. **Tagging is 100% greenfield** — no tag model, column, or endpoint filter. "tag" in the codebase is purely the antd UI component.
4. **DQ dimensions don't exist** — deferred as issue #124. The nearest proxy is the frontend-only `ExpectationCategory`, plus `Check.kind`.
5. **Per-asset scorecard already partially exists** in `AssetSummaryRead`/`asset_view_service`, but it uses a *different* aggregation path (worst_severity rollup) than `dashboard_service`'s health-score/pass-rate formula — two parallel rollup implementations to reconcile if you unify them.
6. **Runners take primitives, not ORM objects** — `run_checks(table, schema, checks, index_columns)`; a new engine plugs in via `_RUNNER_BUILDERS` + a `CheckRunner`/`MonitorRunner` protocol impl, emitting `CheckOutcome`s that map onto `results` columns.

- Header comment (lines 4-8): *"there is no server catalog"* — the backend treats `expectation_type` as a free string title-cased to a GX class. There is a test fixture `backend/tests/fixtures/expectation_catalog.json` and contract test `backend/tests/datasources/test_catalog_gx_contract.py`, but no runtime backend catalog module.

### Dashboard/aggregation service (`backend/app/services/dashboard_service.py`)
- Aggregation is done in **SQL over the persisted `Result.status` column** (never JSONB in Python) — module docstring lines 8-9.
- Health score (ADR 0005): `_PENALTY` weights `pass=0.0, warn=0.5, fail=1.0, critical=2.0`, normalized by `_W_MAX=2.0` → `health_score()` (line 50). `pass_rate()` (line 65) = passed / evaluated-severity-results; **`skip` and `error` are excluded from the denominator** (`_SEVERITY_STATUSES`, line 43).
- **Everything is suite-scoped, NOT asset-scoped.** `dashboard_summary` (line 306) filters by `suite_service.accessible_suite_ids`; `_status_counts` joins `Result→Run` and groups by status (line 143); `_suite_performance` (line 197) computes per-**suite** latest-run health via `DISTINCT ON (suite_id)`. There is **no per-asset aggregation in dashboard_service** — per-asset rollup lives separately in `asset_view_service._roll_up` (which re-implements `worst_severity`/`checks_passed` counting over composing suites' latest runs, using `worst_severity()` from models.py:71 and SEVERITY_RANK, not the dashboard health-score formula).

### Cross-cutting constants (`models.py:37-83`) an alternate engine / scorecard must respect
- `RESULT_STATUSES` = `_RESULT_SEVERITY_TIERS` + `RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES` (line 46). Severity tiers: `SEVERITY_RANK` (line 53), `FAILING_TIERS` = keys worst-last (line 59), `worst_severity()` helper (line 71). Operational statuses include `skip`/`error`/`cancelled`.
- `RUN_STATUSES` = `queued, running, succeeded, failed, cancelled` (line 37).

## Flags vs. your assumptions
1. **No `backend/app/db/models/` directory** — all models are in a single `models.py`.
2. **Suite has no `engine` field** and nothing engine-related; engine = implicit via `connection.type` → runner registry. DQX is prose-only aspiration (unity_catalog docstrings + ADRs), no code.
3. **Tagging is 100% greenfield** — no tag model, column, or endpoint filter. "tag" in the codebase is purely the antd UI component.
4. **DQ dimensions don't exist** — deferred as issue #124. The nearest proxy is the frontend-only `ExpectationCategory`, plus `Check.kind`.
5. **Per-asset scorecard already partially exists** in `AssetSummaryRead`/`asset_view_service`, but it uses a *different* aggregation path (worst_severity rollup) than `dashboard_service`'s health-score/pass-rate formula — two parallel rollup implementations to reconcile if you unify them.
6. **Runners take primitives, not ORM objects** — `run_checks(table, schema, checks, index_columns)`; a new engine plugs in via `_RUNNER_BUILDERS` + a `CheckRunner`/`MonitorRunner` protocol impl, emitting `CheckOutcome`s that map onto `results` columns.

content.text:
All three are feasible — none require reworking the core schema, and the codebase already has deliberate seams pointing at two of them. Here's the assessment, grounded in the current code (no changes made).

---

## 1. Generic "platform-native DQ engine" (DQX, Snowflake DMF, Dataplex…)

**Verdict: feasible, and the seam is already 80% shaped for it — but "native engine" hides two genuinely different integration patterns, and the design should say so explicitly.**

What exists today: `CheckRunner` is a protocol in [base.py](backend/app/datasources/base.py) (`run_checks(table, schema, checks) → SuiteOutcome`), dispatched per connection type via `_RUNNER_BUILDERS` in [registry.py](backend/app/datasources/registry.py). The UC runner's docstrings explicitly name DQX as the swap-in, and the post-v1 roadmap carries it as ADR 0003's v1.1 lane plus the G-g "engine-abstraction watch item" — there's no dedicated GitHub issue yet, which is worth fixing regardless. Notably, **nothing stores an engine choice**: engine is implicit in `connection.type`. Generalizing means an additive `Suite.engine` column (default `'gx'`) and two-key dispatch `(conn_type, engine) → runner builder`, plus a capability registry (which engines each conn type supports, and which check types each engine can translate). That part is small and backward-compatible.

The real fork is in engine *semantics*:

- **Synchronous evaluators (DQX, ad-hoc DMF calls)** fit the existing seam. DQX is rules-in → per-rule outcomes-out, which maps cleanly to `CheckSpec → CheckOutcome`. Caveat: the current UC runner pulls the table into pandas locally; DQX's whole value is Spark-side execution in the workspace, so a proper DQX integration is "submit rules to Databricks and collect results," not "swap the library" — a bigger lift than the docstring implies. Snowflake DMFs *can* also run this way (they're callable in a plain `SELECT`, returning a scalar), which maps beautifully onto the existing `metric_value` column with DataQ applying thresholds — and thresholds are already applied by the run service, not the runner, so that division of labor already works.
- **Warehouse-scheduled monitors (DMF schedules on tables, Dataplex/CloudDQ scans)** do *not* fit `run_checks`. There, the platform runs the checks on its own cadence and DataQ's job is to **provision attachments and ingest results** — structurally the same pull pattern you already built for orchestration polling and warehouse lineage (this branch!). That should be a second seam (think `NativeMonitorProvider`), landing rows into `results` with `metric_value` via the monitor-kind seam, not a `CheckRunner` impl.

One more honest constraint: Dataplex presupposes **BigQuery as a datasource**, which DataQ doesn't have — that's the dominant cost of that row, not the engine seam. My recommendation: write an ADR that (a) adds `Suite.engine` + the capability registry, (b) names the two patterns above, and (c) sequences DQX (sync, UC, already-promised) first, ad-hoc DMF second (cheap, high leverage since Snowflake is your flagship datasource), scheduled-DMF ingest third, Dataplex only with a BigQuery datasource decision. Per-engine, the recurring cost is the check-type translation matrix and normalizing outcomes into `RESULT_STATUSES` — that matrix is the thing to keep small and explicit.

## 2. Tagging

**Verdict: feasible, fully greenfield, and cheap — the main work is one design decision.**

There is no tag concept anywhere in the data model today (every "tag" hit in the frontend is the antd `<Tag>` component). All candidate entities — `Asset`, `Connection`, `Suite`, `Check` — have UUID PKs, so both classic shapes work:

- **Polymorphic**: one `tags` table + one `entity_tags(entity_type, entity_id, tag_id)` table. Fewer tables, but no real FK to the tagged entity — orphan rows on delete become your problem. Given the suite-delete FK cascade bug you already got burned by (#540), I'd avoid this.
- **Per-entity join tables** (recommended): `tags` + `suite_tags` / `asset_tags` / `connection_tags` / `check_tags`, each with real FKs and `ON DELETE CASCADE`. Four small tables, referential integrity for free, purely additive migration — clean under the backward-compat rule.

Recommended v1 scope: flat, workspace-shared labels (unique name, free-create by editors), `tags: [str]` added to read models, tag assignment via the existing PATCH endpoints, and a `tag=` filter param on the list endpoints. Note the list endpoints have essentially no filtering today (`/suites` filters only by `connection_id`, `/assets` not at all), so the filter is new-but-trivial WHERE clauses. Frontend is a natural fit — antd `Select mode="tags"` is already in use elsewhere.

Two design cautions: **don't conflate this with #433** (warehouse-native PII/PHI tags) — that's ingesting Snowflake/UC object tags as classification, a different trust model; but if you think you'll ever ingest those, shape `tags` as optionally key:value (like Snowflake object tags) rather than repainting later. And **don't implement DQ dimensions as tags** — dimensions want to be a structured field (see below), tags are the free-form axis. Effort: roughly one backend PR + one UI PR + one filters PR.

## 3. Asset DQ scorecard (dimension-based, weighted)

**Verdict: feasible and well-supported — about 60% of the substrate exists. The one hard prerequisite is exactly your open issue [#124](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/124) (DQ-dimension classification on checks).**

What already exists:
- A per-asset rollup: `AssetSummaryRead` already carries `checks_total`, `checks_passed`, `worst_severity`, and operational flags, computed by `asset_view_service._roll_up` over composing suites' latest runs. `Run.asset_id` is stamped at dispatch, so per-asset aggregation over `results` is a straight SQL join — no schema change needed for the "how many passing" half.
- A scoring formula: `dashboard_service` has the ADR-0005 severity-weighted health score (`pass=0, warn=0.5, fail=1, critical=2`) and `pass_rate` — but suite-scoped, and it's a **parallel implementation** to the asset rollup. Unifying those two paths is a prerequisite refactor worth doing anyway.

What's missing:
- **Dimensions (#124).** No dimension exists on `Check`; the closest thing is the frontend-only `ExpectationCategory` in `expectationCatalog.ts` (there is deliberately no backend catalog). Recommended shape: a `dimension` column on `Check` with a backend derivation function as the default — `kind=freshness` → Timeliness, `kind=volume` → Completeness/Volume, null/missing expectations → Completeness, `unique` → Uniqueness, range/regex/set → Validity, `comparison` → Consistency, `schema_drift` → Schema — plus a stored per-check override. Derivation-with-override means existing checks classify themselves on day one and the migration stays additive.
- **Weights.** Recommend v1 = per-dimension weights only (workspace default, optional per-asset override, stored as config/JSONB) — score = Σ(dimension pass-rate × weight)/Σweights, reusing the severity penalty within a dimension. Per-check weights are a lot of UI and explanation for marginal value; defer them.
- The genuinely valuable half is the **coverage view**: "this asset has zero Uniqueness or Timeliness checks" is more actionable than the score itself, and it falls out of the same dimension column for free.

One design decision to make consciously: asset visibility is derived from suite grants, and your #845 lesson was "don't ship a lie to fix a leak." A per-viewer score (computed only over suites the viewer can see) gives different users different numbers for the same asset; a workspace-true score computed over *all* composing suites is honest but must not break down suites the viewer can't see. I'd recommend the workspace-true score with hidden contributions shown as redacted counts — consistent with the anonymous-lineage-node precedent you already set.

**Sequencing across all three:** #124 dimensions → scorecard → weights, with tagging fully parallel (and tags later becoming the grouping axis for scorecard views, e.g. "score by domain:finance"). The native-engine work is independent, and its `metric_value`-producing DMF/Dataplex ingests would feed the same scorecard once both exist.

Happy to turn any of these into an ADR draft or file the issues (a dedicated DQX/native-engine issue is missing today; #124 would want a rescope note linking it to the scorecard) — say the word.

content.text: What do you think the real life benefits of these features? And does DataQ needs them?

Honest take, per feature — including where I'd say "not yet."

## 1. Platform-native engines — real benefit, wrong moment (except one slice)

The genuine real-life benefits are:

- **Pushdown.** Your UC, flat-file, and Iceberg runners pull data into pandas on a small container. That's fine for demo tables and fatal for a 500M-row production table. Native engines run checks where the data lives — this is the *actual* long-term answer to the scale problem you've already filed as #595 (G-b).
- **Meeting customers where they are.** An enterprise on Snowflake may already have DMFs attached to critical tables. A tool that *ingests* those instead of asking teams to re-author everything in GX has a much easier adoption story — "we show you the quality signal your warehouse already computes" beats "please duplicate your checks in our tool."
- **De-risking the GX pin** (your own G-g watch item): the more engines behind the seam, the less the product's fate rides on one fast-drifting dependency.
- **Streaming** (DQX): GX is batch-only; DLT/streaming checks are simply out of reach without it.

Does DataQ need it? **Mostly not yet.** DataQ has no users with 500M-row tables, no streaming workloads, and no BigQuery datasource — so DQX and Dataplex are bets on future users, not solutions to present pain. And #595's sampling approach buys most of the near-term scale headroom far cheaper. The exception is **ad-hoc DMF invocation on Snowflake**: it's cheap (a `SELECT`, mapping a scalar to `metric_value`, thresholds already applied outside the runner), it's on your flagship datasource, and it's a real differentiator line in a marketplace listing. I'd do that slice, file the rest as an ADR-backed direction, and not build DQX until a real streaming/Databricks user shows up.

## 2. Tagging — real benefit only at a scale DataQ hasn't reached

Tags earn their keep when there are hundreds of suites/assets or multiple teams: filter by `domain:finance`, route alerts by `criticality:high`, scope retention or reviews by tag. Those are genuine operational benefits — in an org.

DataQ today is single-tenant with a few dozen entities and demo users. At that size, tags are a filter for a list that fits on one screen. Nobody's real problem gets solved this quarter. The honest verdict: **nice-to-have, defer until there's a driver** — and the driver to watch for is not "finding things" but *something else keying off tags*: alert routing, scorecard grouping, or the #433 PII-classification ingest. When one of those becomes real, build tags as its substrate (that's also when you'll know whether flat labels or key:value is right, instead of guessing). Building it now is cheap but would be shelfware.

## 3. Asset scorecard — this is the one DataQ actually needs

This is the odd one out: it's not a bet on future users, it's the capstone of what you just built. Real-life benefits:

- **It's the artifact non-engineers consume.** Check-level results are for the person who wrote the checks. A per-asset score is what a data consumer looks at before trusting a table, and what a lead looks at to decide where DQ effort goes. Every commercial DQ product (Monte Carlo, Soda, Atlan) converges on this view because it's the thing that gets shown in meetings.
- **Coverage gaps are the killer feature, not the score.** "`mart_order_revenue` has zero freshness or uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable — and your own CLAUDE.md notes most real incidents are freshness/volume, not value-level. A scorecard that surfaces *unchecked dimensions* turns DataQ from "runs the checks you wrote" into "tells you which checks you should have written." That's the 'GX runner → DQ platform' leap in one screen.
- **It compounds with what just shipped.** Assets, lineage, incidents (G-d) built the asset page; the scorecard is what makes that page the destination. It also demos better than anything else on this list — relevant given the marketplace ambitions.

The caveat I'd design around: weighted scores invite endless weight-tuning debates and can become vanity metrics. So sequence it as **#124 dimensions + coverage view first, equal-weight score second, custom weights only if someone asks** — the weights machinery is the least valuable third of the feature and the most configurable-surface to maintain.

**Bottom line:** scorecard (via #124) is a clear yes and I'd schedule it; ad-hoc DMF is a cheap, differentiating yes; tagging and the general engine framework are "real benefits, wrong time" — file them with their triggers written down (multi-team/routing need for tags; a streaming or BigQuery user for the engines) so they activate on evidence rather than speculation.

input.command:
gh issue create \
  --title "feat: tagging for suites / assets / connections / checks (workspace labels + list filters)" \
  --label enhancement --label priority/P3 \
  --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" \
  --body "## Summary
Introduce first-class tags (workspace-shared labels) on the four core entities — \`Suite\`, \`Asset\`, \`Connection\`, \`Check\` — with tag filters on the list endpoints and tag chips/pickers in the UI.

Feasibility assessed 2026-07-17: fully greenfield — no tag concept exists in the data model today (every \"tag\" hit in the frontend is the antd \`<Tag>\` UI component). All four entities have UUID PKs, so the association model is straightforward.

## Deliberately parked in Backlog — activation triggers
At the current entity count (single tenant, a few dozen suites/assets) tags are a filter for a list that fits on one screen. Pull this into a week when one of these becomes real, and build tags **as that feature's substrate**:
- multi-team / multi-domain usage (discovery: \`domain:finance\`, \`team:core-data\`)
- alert routing keyed off tags (\`criticality:high\` → different channel/severity)
- asset-scorecard grouping views (\"score by domain\") — see the scorecard issue
- #433 warehouse-tag ingestion for PII/PHI classification (related but distinct: warehouse-native tags are a *classification source*, not user labels — don't conflate, but the key:value shape below keeps the door open)

## Recommended approach (from the feasibility pass)
- **Schema:** normalized \`tags\` table + **per-entity join tables** (\`suite_tags\`, \`asset_tags\`, \`connection_tags\`, \`check_tags\`) with real FKs + \`ON DELETE CASCADE\`. Explicitly *not* a polymorphic \`entity_tags(entity_type, entity_id)\` table — no DB-level FK means orphan cleanup on entity delete becomes our problem (see the #540 suite-delete cascade lesson).
- **Shape:** flat labels v1 (unique name, free-create by editors); model the name column so a later \`key:value\` form (à la Snowflake object tags) is additive, not a repaint.
- **API:** additive — \`tags: [str]\` on read models, assignment via existing PATCH endpoints, \`tag=\` query param on list endpoints (note: list-filter infra is thin today — \`/suites\` filters only by \`connection_id\`, \`/assets\` has none — new-but-trivial WHERE clauses).
- **Authz:** tag *assignments* visible with the entity (suite-grant scoped); the tag *vocabulary* is workspace-shared.
- **Frontend:** antd \`Select mode=\"tags\"\` already in use elsewhere — natural fit.
- Migrations purely additive (backward-compatible per CONTRIBUTING).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] \`tags\` + four join tables, additive Alembic migration (up+down tested)
- [ ] Tag CRUD + assignment via PATCH on all four entities; tags in read models
- [ ] \`tag=\` filter on \`/suites\` and \`/assets\` list endpoints (repeatable param = AND semantics, documented)
- [ ] Deleting an entity cascades its tag assignments; deleting a tag detaches it everywhere (test both)
- [ ] UI: tag chips on list rows + tag picker on entity edit; filter control on Suites + Assets lists
- [ ] Authz test: a user without suite access cannot enumerate that suite's tag assignments
- [ ] Docs: user-docs page section + feature-matrix row" \
  2>&1

input.command:
gh issue create \
  --title "feat: asset DQ scorecard — per-dimension coverage + weighted score on the asset page" \
  --label enhancement --label priority/P2 \
  --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" \
  --body "## Summary
Add a DQ scorecard to the asset detail page (and a score/coverage column on the assets list): per-DQ-dimension view of **which check types are configured** (coverage) and **how many are passing** (score), rolled up over the asset's composing suites' latest runs.

**Depends on #124** (DQ-dimension classification on checks — same milestone). The scorecard is #124's consumer: dimensions land on \`Check\`, the scorecard aggregates by them.

## Why (feasibility assessed 2026-07-17)
- The check-level view serves the check author; a per-asset score is what a data *consumer* looks at before trusting a table, and what a lead uses to aim DQ effort. It makes the asset page (G-d: #757/#760/#773) the destination view.
- **Coverage gaps are the killer half, not the score**: \"this asset has zero Timeliness or Uniqueness checks\" is immediately actionable — and most real incidents are freshness/volume (CLAUDE.md §5), i.e. exactly the dimensions most likely to be uncovered.
- ~60% of the substrate exists: \`AssetSummaryRead\` already rolls up \`checks_total\`/\`checks_passed\`/\`worst_severity\` (asset_view_service._roll_up); \`Run.asset_id\` is stamped at dispatch so per-asset SQL aggregation over \`results\` needs no schema change; the ADR-0005 severity-weighted health formula exists in dashboard_service.

## Scope decisions (from the feasibility pass)
- **v1 = coverage view + equal-weight score.** Custom per-dimension weights (workspace default + per-asset override) only if demanded; per-check weights explicitly out of scope — most-configurable-surface, least value.
- **Score semantics:** severity-weighted pass rate per dimension (reuse the ADR-0005 penalty map), computed over composing suites' **latest runs** (same semantics as \`_roll_up\`); \`skip\`/\`error\` excluded from denominators (matches \`dashboard_service._SEVERITY_STATUSES\`).
- **Authz:** workspace-true score (same number for every viewer who can see the asset), with contributions from suites outside the viewer's grants shown as **redacted counts, not omitted** — the #845/#846 anonymous-neighbour precedent; a per-viewer score would silently disagree between users.
- **Prerequisite refactor:** \`asset_view_service._roll_up\` and \`dashboard_service\` are two parallel rollup implementations — unify the shared status/severity math before adding a third consumer.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] #124 landed: \`Check.dimension\` (derived default + stored override) available to aggregation
- [ ] Rollup unification: one shared severity/status aggregation helper used by dashboard_service, asset_view_service, and the scorecard (no third parallel formula)
- [ ] \`GET /assets/{id}\` extended with per-dimension scorecard: \`{dimension, checks_total, checks_passing, score}\` + uncovered-dimensions list
- [ ] Redaction: non-grantee contributions appear as counts only; test pins that browse/detail/scorecard agree (one-rule pattern from #847)
- [ ] Asset page scorecard panel (coverage gaps visually distinct from failing dimensions) + assets-list score column with sort
- [ ] Empty states honest: no checks ≠ score 100 (render \"no coverage\", not a green tick); stale runs surfaced via \`last_run_at\`
- [ ] Tests: SQL-level aggregation over seeded multi-suite/multi-dimension fixtures incl. skip/error exclusion + operational-failure axis (#803 split respected)
- [ ] Docs: user-docs asset page section + feature-matrix row" \

content.text:
Both issues are filed and mirrored:

- **#888 — Tagging** → `v1.1 Backlog`, `enhancement` + `priority/P3`, with the activation triggers (multi-team use, tag-keyed alert routing, scorecard grouping, #433 ingest) written into the body so it pulls forward on evidence, not vibes. Added to the Roadmap project (week field left unset, like other backlog items — the field has no Backlog option).
- **#889 — Asset DQ scorecard** → `v1.1 Week 4`, `enhancement` + `priority/P2`, declared as dependent on #124 (already in W4), with the scope decisions baked in: coverage-first, equal weights v1, workspace-true score with redacted non-grantee contributions, and the rollup-unification prerequisite. Project field set to `W4 schema_drift`.

---

Now, item 1 — the native-engine direction. Let me open with something I noticed while writing up the feasibility that changes the shape of the discussion:

**The DMF slice may not need the engine seam at all.** I originally framed "ad-hoc DMF" as a small case of the generic engine abstraction. But look at what it actually is: run a `SELECT SNOWFLAKE.CORE.NULL_COUNT(...)`, get back a scalar, apply DataQ thresholds, store `metric_value`. That's not a competing *evaluator of expectations* — it's a **native metric measurement**, and DataQ already has a purpose-built seam for exactly that shape: the ADR-0012 monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, `metric_value`, thresholds applied outside the runner — the same seam freshness/volume rode in on). Modeled as `check.kind = 'native_metric'` with kwargs naming the DMF and column, it's a new monitor kind on the Snowflake connection — no `Suite.engine` column, no two-key registry, no ADR-scale abstraction work. That makes the one slice with a real near-term case dramatically cheaper, and it defers the whole "engine toggle" question to the first true second *evaluator* (DQX).

Which reframes the generic-engine question into three separable decisions:

1. **The engine toggle (`Suite.engine`, two-key dispatch)** — only needed when a second synchronous evaluator actually arrives, i.e. DQX. And DQX's honest prerequisite isn't the seam, it's **remote execution**: today's UC runner pulls data into pandas locally; DQX's value is Spark-side evaluation, which means DataQ learning to submit work to a Databricks workspace and collect results — a new architectural capability. One flag to check before ever committing: DQX is a Databricks *Labs* project, and Labs licensing varies — some are MIT, some ship the custom Databricks license, which would trip ADR 0031's no-source-available rule. Worth a 10-minute license check before it stays on any roadmap.

2. **Scheduled-native ingest (DMF schedules, Dataplex scans)** — the "warehouse runs the checks, DataQ ingests" pattern. This has a genuine schema tension I glossed over earlier: `results.run_id` is a NOT-NULL FK, but a warehouse-scheduled measurement has no DataQ run. So ingest means either synthesizing runs per poll sweep (keeps one results table, muddies run semantics) or a sibling measurements table (clean, but the scorecard/trends must union two sources). That's the real design question for this pattern — worth settling in an ADR *before* anyone builds it.

3. **The ADR itself** — I'd still write it now, cheaply: it discharges the G-g engine-abstraction watch item, records the native-metric-vs-evaluator distinction, and pins the triggers (DQX ⇐ a real streaming/Databricks user + license check passing; Dataplex ⇐ a BigQuery datasource decision; scheduled-DMF ingest ⇐ a user who already has DMFs attached).

So my proposal for item 1: **ADR now, `native_metric` DMF slice as the only near-term build (it could even ride the existing monitor-kind machinery in a normal week), everything else trigger-gated.**

The questions I'd want your take on: does the `native_metric` framing sit right with you, or do you see DMFs as a first-class engine (e.g., because you'd eventually want DataQ *creating* DMF attachments in the warehouse, not just calling them)? And for the ingest pattern — synthetic runs or a sibling table, gut feeling? That choice leaks into the scorecard's data model, so it's worth having an opinion before #889 lands.

content.text: I want DMF as a engine too. Same with the DQX and Dataplex. But these features should only be available if there is a connection. like DMF ties to Snowflake, DQX ties to Databricks, Dataplex tries to BigQuery. So it will be a added feature of the connection. If the user create a snowflake connection, we can offer a DMF based check engine. If there is no snowflake connection, there will be a no DMF. what do you think about this?

I think connection-anchored engine availability is the right call — it matches both the architecture and the mental model users already have. Let me affirm it and then push on four refinements it needs to survive contact with the codebase.

**Why it's right.** Engine dispatch is *already* keyed by connection type (`_RUNNER_BUILDERS` in [registry.py](backend/app/datasources/registry.py)) — your model just extends that to a capability map: every datasource connection offers `gx`; a Snowflake connection additionally offers `dmf`; UC offers `dqx`; a future BigQuery connection offers `dataplex`. No global engine setting, no engine config page — the connection *is* the capability surface, and the check editor simply shows what the suite's connection supports. It also cleanly kills the Dataplex ambiguity: no BigQuery connection type, no Dataplex, full stop. And I'll concede the point from last round: with engines as first-class (especially if DataQ later *manages* DMF attachments in the warehouse, not just calls them), modeling DMF as a mere `native_metric` kind would have been a dead end. The useful residue of that idea survives anyway: DMF outcomes are metric-shaped, so they ride the existing `metric_value` + threshold semantics without new result plumbing.

**Refinement 1 — put `engine` on the check, not the suite.** Your framing ("the connection offers an engine") still needs a granularity decision. If `engine` lives on the suite, a Snowflake suite is all-GX or all-DMF — and the realistic suite is *mostly GX expectations plus a few DMF metrics*, forcing users to split suites artificially. Check-level `engine` (default `'gx'`, validated against the connection's capability set) avoids that, and the run path already has the pattern: a single run today partitions checks and sends expectations through the `CheckRunner` and monitor kinds through the `MonitorRunner`. Partitioning by engine is the same move. The CLAUDE.md §5 sketch ("`engine: gx | dqx` toggle on UC suites") predates this discussion — the check-level shape supersedes it, and the ADR should say so explicitly.

**Refinement 2 — type gates the *offer*; the connection instance should validate the *reality*.** "Snowflake connection ⇒ DMF" is the right default, but DMF actually requires Enterprise Edition and specific privileges; DQX requires the workspace to permit job/serverless execution. So: connection *type* unlocks the engine in the UI, and the connection **test/re-auth flow probes actual availability**, cached as a capability flag on the connection — so the editor can say "DMF unavailable on this connection: requires Enterprise edition" instead of letting the user author checks that fail at run time. That can be phase 2 (v1 = gate by type, fail with a clear `error`-status result), but design the capability flag in from the start.

**Refinement 3 — keep `engine` orthogonal to `kind`.** `kind` says *what's measured* (expectation, freshness, volume, comparison…); `engine` says *who evaluates it*. These must stay independent axes: Snowflake's `FRESHNESS`/`ROW_COUNT` system DMFs are alternate evaluators of your existing `freshness`/`volume` kinds, not new kinds. Getting this wrong (e.g., a `dmf_freshness` kind) would fork the scorecard's dimension mapping and the monitor-kind seam. Corollary: each engine carries a **supported-matrix** (which kinds/check types it can evaluate), which is per-check information — another reason engine belongs on the check. Practical consequence: the expectation catalog — currently frontend-only by design — becomes engine-aware, which nudges toward the backend catalog that #124's dimension work already wants. Those two should probably land as one catalog story.

**Refinement 4 — the lifecycle edges need explicit answers.** Tying capability to a connection instance creates edges the ADR must cover: connection deleted or re-pointed → checks whose engine is no longer offered (validate on save; at run time, land as the existing `error` operational status, never a silent skip); suite export/import re-mapped to a connection lacking the engine (import-time validation with a clear per-check report). None are hard; all are the kind of thing that bites in prod if left implicit.

**Bottom line:** I'd adopt your model verbatim as the ADR's core decision — *"engines are capabilities of connections; GX is universal; native engines are unlocked by connection type and validated per instance"* — with `check.engine` as the storage shape, the capability probe as phase 2, and DQX/Dataplex still trigger-gated (DQX additionally needs the remote-execution decision and the Labs license check; Dataplex needs BigQuery-as-datasource first). The near-term build under this model is unchanged in cost: the DMF engine on Snowflake connections, mapping the system DMFs (`NULL_COUNT`, `DUPLICATE_COUNT`, `UNIQUE_COUNT`, `ROW_COUNT`, `FRESHNESS`) onto existing kinds.

Want me to draft the ADR capturing this (connection-anchored engines + the four refinements + triggers), and file the umbrella issue for the DMF engine build? That would give item 1 the same paper trail items 2 and 3 now have.

1	# ADR 0030 — Apache Iceberg support: native `pyiceberg` read (v2) behind a self-contained `iceberg` datasource
2	
3	- **Status:** Accepted (spike decision, 2026-07-07; native impl deferred — see Consequences)
4	- **Date:** 2026-07-07
5	- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
6	- **Related:** [0003](0003-gx-only-for-v1.md) (GX-only; DQX swap seam), [0011](0011-extensibility-seams-for-deferred-integrations.md) (second-impl-deferred seams — `CheckRunner`/`ConnectionAdapter`), [0012](0012-monitor-kind-seam.md) (freshness/schema-drift monitor kinds this feeds), [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md)/[0013](0013-marketplace-distribution-and-anti-lock-in.md) (anti-lock-in), [0015](0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) (two-connection model — settled 2026-07-11 at the *check* grain only; Option B's connection→connection ref stays deferred, see its §6). Issue [#286](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/286).
7	
8	## Context
9	
10	Apache Iceberg is increasingly the default open table format on Databricks (Unity Catalog managed tables), Snowflake, and object storage (S3 Tables / ADLS + Polaris). #286 asks whether DataQ should read Iceberg tables natively. The spike (this session) answered two prior questions first, because they collapse most of the scope:
11	
12	1. **Does reading Iceberg through a query engine need new DataQ code?** No. `SnowflakeCheckRunner` (`add_table_asset` → SQL pushdown, `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py`) and `UnityCatalogCheckRunner` (`pd.read_sql_table` over the SQL Warehouse, `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py`) talk **SQL to the engine, never to the file format**. Whether an identifier resolves to a native FDN/Delta table or an Iceberg table (`CREATE ICEBERG TABLE … CATALOG_SOURCE=OBJECT_STORE`, or a Databricks foreign/UniForm table) is transparent to the connector, GX, and DataQ. A user can point a suite at an engine-registered Iceberg table **today, with zero new code**, under the existing `snowflake` / `unity_catalog` connection.
13	
14	2. **Is an Iceberg table just a parquet file (reuse `FlatFileCheckRunner`)?** No. `FlatFileCheckRunner` reads **one object** (`pd.read_parquet`, `backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py:118`). An Iceberg table is a *table*: a metadata pointer → manifest list → manifests → many data files across snapshots/partitions. Reading the raw parquet files naïvely gives **wrong answers** — stale files from expired snapshots (over-count), ignored v2 delete files (rows that don't exist in the table), and per-file physical schema instead of the table's field-ID-reconciled logical schema. Correct reads require an Iceberg client (`pyiceberg`: current snapshot → apply deletes → reconcile schema-by-ID → DataFrame).
15	
16	So the *only* genuinely new capability #286 can add is a **native read directly from object storage with no query engine in the loop** — `pyiceberg` scan → pandas DataFrame → the existing `gx_runner`, exactly the `FlatFileCheckRunner`/`UnityCatalogCheckRunner` output shape (and the same shape DQX consumes, ADR 0003).
17	
18	## Decision
19	
20	### 1. Add native Iceberg as a new datasource; keep engine-level reads as the free zero-code path
21	
22	- **Engine-level (Snowflake iceberg table / Databricks foreign or UniForm catalog) — supported now, no code.** Documented as the "already works" path; it costs the engine's warehouse compute and only exposes what the engine surfaces as SQL.
23	- **Native `pyiceberg` — the new build.** `IcebergCheckRunner` reads a table via `pyiceberg` (`catalog.load_table(...).scan()`) and hands the frame to `gx_runner.run_expectations` — thin, like `UnityCatalogCheckRunner`. Materialize via `.to_arrow()` → Arrow-backed pandas (**not** the bare `.to_pandas()` shortcut, which drops to numpy dtypes — keep parity with `FlatFileCheckRunner`'s `dtype_backend="pyarrow"`) for the exact-expectation path; use `.to_arrow_batch_reader()` for the streamable monitor/aggregate path. See #716 for the materialization detail. Payoff over engine-level: **no warehouse compute**, and **direct access to snapshot + schema history** — the natural feed for the reserved `freshness` / `schema_drift` monitor kinds (ADR 0012).
24	
25	Routing is unchanged in shape: `registry.build_check_runner` dispatches on `connection.type`. `iceberg` is a new sibling entry next to `snowflake` / `adls_gen2` / `s3` / `unity_catalog`. **csv/parquet flat-file reads are untouched** — a `.parquet` *object* stays flat-file; an Iceberg *table* is addressed as a table (namespace.table or metadata location), never inferred from an extension. No rerouting of parquet through Iceberg.
26	
27	### 2. Format-version 2 baseline; v3 deferred behind a capability gate
28	
29	**v2 now.** Iceberg format-version 2 is GA and read everywhere that matters — Snowflake Iceberg tables are GA at v2, Spark/Databricks read v2 universally, Delta UniForm emits v2 (`delta.enableIcebergCompatV2`), and `pyiceberg`'s v2 read path (position/equality deletes, schema-by-ID) is mature.
30	
31	**v3 deferred.** Iceberg v3 (deletion vectors, row lineage, multi-arg/variant transforms) is new and **unevenly supported across engines**, and `pyiceberg`'s v3 support is still maturing. v3-only features go behind a capability flag when engine support converges; v2 stays the baseline. Revisit when the ecosystem catches up (own follow-up, not this build).
32	
33	### 3. `iceberg` connection = self-contained (Option A), **not** a reference to an ADLS/S3 connection
34	
35	An Iceberg connection needs two things the flat-file connection lacks: a **catalog pointer** (where the metadata lives) and **storage credentials** (to fetch data files). Two models were considered:
36	
37	| | **A. Self-contained (chosen)** | **B. Two-connection reference** |
38	|---|---|---|
39	| Shape | `iceberg` connection carries catalog config **+ its own** storage credential (one `config` + one `secret_ref`) | `iceberg` connection carries catalog config **+ a reference** to an existing `adls_gen2`/`s3` connection for storage |
40	| Fits current code | **Yes** — drops into `build_check_runner` with no new plumbing; matches the "one `secret_ref` per connection" invariant every adapter already holds | **No** — the model has no connection→connection FK; runner builders take a single config/secret |
41	| Lifecycle | Independent — deleting the flat-file connection can't strand the Iceberg one | Coupled — shared credential + the cross-connection FK entangle lifecycles |
42	| Cost | Storage credential is duplicated if the user also has that ADLS/S3 connection | Cleaner credential reuse |
43	
44	**Chosen: Option A.** It ships inside the existing `ConnectionAdapter`/`CheckRunner` seams with zero new cross-connection machinery, and it keeps lifecycles independent — which matches the real usage pattern: a user may register an ADLS path for **flat files and Iceberg tables both**, then later delete the flat-file connection and use that storage **only for Iceberg going forward**. Under Option A that deletion is clean — the `iceberg` connection owns its own storage credential and is unaffected, and the existing cascade-delete on the connection FK (ADR 0020) removes only the flat-file connection's own suites/checks. Under Option B the same action would either strand the Iceberg connection's storage credential or cascade into it. Credential duplication is the accepted cost.
45	
46	#### 3a. Amendment (2026-07-12): "one `secret_ref` per connection" was **wrong for a SQL catalog** — #754 / #826
47	
48	Option A assumed one credential per connection. A **SQL catalog** needs *two*: the storage key **and** the catalog DB password. With the single `secret_ref` slot taken by the storage key, the catalog password had nowhere legitimate to go — so it was smuggled inline into `config.catalog_uri`.
49	
50	`config` is **not** a secret. It is persisted in plaintext JSONB, returned verbatim by `GET /connections`, and — because ADR 0034 derives the Iceberg asset's OpenLineage namespace from `catalog_uri` — it was copied into `assets.namespace`, **rendered as label text in the UI** (including as a tree-folder name, beside a Copy button), shipped to third-party catalogs inside a lineage query string, and logged in plaintext. A credential in `config` leaks *by construction*.
51	
52	**Amended decision.** A connection may name **additional** secrets in config, by convention:
53	
54	- `config.<x>_secret_name` holds a **SecretStore key name** (an identifier, not a credential — safe in plaintext config). Iceberg uses `catalog_secret_name`.
55	- `catalog_uri` must ship **credential-less** (username kept — it is an identifier). `IcebergConfig` now **rejects** a URI carrying a password, pointing the author at the secret slot.
56	- The **caller** resolves every `*_secret_name` and passes the values to the adapter as keyword arguments; the credential is injected into the URI at catalog-load time and never persisted. This preserves the ADR 0011 seam invariant — *adapters never touch the SecretStore* — while scaling past one credential.
57	- The namespace is derived from the **stripped** URI, which additionally makes the asset identity **stable across a password rotation** (previously, rotating the password silently forked the asset into a new row, orphaning its lineage and incidents).
58	
59	Migration `c9d0e1f2a3b4` repairs rows written before the guard, updating `assets.namespace` **in place** so `assets.id` — and every FK to it — survives.
